name: Hooked Model
overview: The Hooked Model, proposed by Nir Eyal in his 2014 book 'Hooked: How to Build Habit-Forming Products', is a four-step cyclical framework (Trigger -> Action -> Variable Reward -> Investment) used to design products that build user habits through repeated cycles. It synthesizes B.J. Fogg's behavioral model, B.F. Skinner's variable-ratio reinforcement schedules, and dopamine/anticipation research, and is widely cited as the playbook behind Instagram, Pinterest, Twitter and Duolingo-style retention design.
originator_or_company: Nir Eyal (Israeli-American author, former Stanford Graduate School of Business and Hasso Plattner Institute of Design lecturer). Co-author Ryder Carroll on later editions. Eyal previously founded ad-tech companies AdNectar and Sunshine Business; runs the Nir & Far blog and has authored the follow-up books 'Indistractable' (2019) and 'Hooked' (2014, Portfolio/Penguin).
key_resources: [Nir Eyal - The Hooked Model: How to Manufacture Desire in 4 Steps](https://www.nirandfar.com/how-to-manufacture-desire/)
[Nir Eyal - Variable Rewards: Want To Hook Users? Drive Them Crazy](https://www.nirandfar.com/want-to-hook-your-users-drive-them-crazy/)
[Nir Eyal - The Morality of Manipulation / Manipulation Matrix](https://www.nirandfar.com/the-art-of-manipulation/)
[Hooked: How to Build Habit-Forming Products (Portfolio/Penguin, 2014) - book on Amazon](https://www.amazon.com/Hooked-How-Build-Habit-Forming-Products/dp/1591847788)
[A Case Study on Applications of the Hook Model in Software Products (MDPI 2023)](https://www.mdpi.com/2674-113X/2/2/14)

[Combining the Fogg Behavioural Model and Hook Model to Improve Study Habits (ResearchGate)](https://www.researchgate.net/publication/303223616_Combining_The_Fogg_Behavioural_Model_And_Hook_Model_To_Design_Features_In_A_Persuasive_App_To_Improve_Study_Habits)
[Amplitude - The Hook Model: Retain Users by Creating Habit-Forming Products](https://amplitude.com/blog/the-hook-model)
[UXmatters - Book Review: Hooked: How to Build Habit-Forming Products](https://www.uxmatters.com/mt/archives/2021/06/book-review-hooked-how-to-build-habit-forming-products.php)
[Per Axbom - How Nir Eyal's habit books are dangerous](https://axbom.com/nir-eyal-habit-danger/)
core_mechanics: The Hooked Model is a four-phase loop the user is intended to traverse repeatedly until an internal trigger replaces external prompts and the behavior becomes habitual. (1) Trigger: the cue that initiates the behavior. External triggers include push notifications, emails, app icons, calls-to-action; internal triggers are emotions or routines (boredom, loneliness, FOMO, post-meal moment) that pair with the product after enough cycles. The strategic goal is to migrate users from external to internal triggers. (2) Action: the simplest behavior done in anticipation of a reward. Eyal explicitly imports B.J. Fogg's B = MAP (Behavior = Motivation x Ability x Prompt) here and prescribes minimizing friction (one-tap, one-scroll, one-swipe) so the action requires almost no conscious thought. (3) Variable Reward: the payoff, made unpredictable in timing and magnitude in line with Skinner's variable-ratio reinforcement schedules. Eyal categorizes variable rewards into three types - Rewards of the Tribe (social validation: likes, comments, leaderboards), Rewards of the Hunt (pursuit of resources/information: scrolling feeds, search results, treasure chests), and Rewards of the Self (intrinsic mastery, completion, finishing): these map to dopamine release in anticipation of reward, not the reward itself (Schultz 1997). (4) Investment: the user puts in time, data, effort, social capital, content, followers, or money, which both increases switching cost and loads the next trigger (e.g. follow someone -> their next post is now an external trigger to you). Investments differ from Action because they expect a future return, not an immediate one. Repeating the loop strengthens the internal trigger -> behavior association until the product is consulted reflexively. Eyal pairs the model with the 'Habit Zone' (high frequency + high perceived utility) and the 'Manipulation Matrix' ethics filter (Facilitator / Peddler / Entertainer / Dealer).
designer_or_researcher_voice: Nir Eyal (Hooked, 2014): 'A habit is when not doing an action causes a bit of pain.'

Nir Eyal (Nir & Far, 'Variable Rewards'): 'Variable rewards are one of the most powerful tools companies implement to hook users... what the Skinner Box experiments revealed is that what draws us to act is not the sensation we receive from the reward itself, but the need to alleviate the craving for that reward.'
Nir Eyal (Hooked): 'Companies that successfully change behaviors present users with an implicit choice between their old way of doing things and a new, more convenient way to fulfill existing needs.'

Nir Eyal (TechCrunch, 'The Art of Manipulation', 2012): 'The Manipulation Matrix does not answer for the morality of an act, just whether the technology is something the designer would use. The Facilitator builds something they themselves would use and that materially improves the lives of users; the Dealer does neither.'

Nir Eyal (Indistractable, 2019, walking back): 'It's our responsibility to make sure we're not abusing these products... the way to deal with distraction is not to blame the technology, but to understand the internal triggers that drive us to it.'

Per Axbom (critic, axbom.com): 'Eyal's books are dangerous because they teach designers to deliberately manufacture compulsive behavior and then, when challenged, redirect responsibility onto the user.'
metrics_or_evidence: The Hooked Model itself is a synthesis framework, so direct effect-size evidence is indirect, drawn from the underlying psychology (Skinner's variable-ratio schedules, Schultz 1997 dopamine prediction-error work, Lally et al. 2010 habit formation ~66 days) and from product case studies. Reported product-level outcomes attributed to Hooked-style design include: Duolingo Streak Wager +14% Day-14 retention, Streak Freeze -21% churn for at-risk users, XP leaderboards +40% engagement, Daily Quests +25% DAU (Duolingo case studies via StriveCloud / TheProductBrief, 2024-2025). Instagram, Pinterest, Twitter and Slack are the canonical examples Eyal cites in the book, but no controlled trial isolates the Hooked Model from confounds. Academic uptake: the MDPI 2023 case study (Karahasanovic & Yao) catalogues Hook-model elements across major social/learning apps; the Fogg+Hook study app (ResearchGate 2016) reports qualitative engagement gains in a small university cohort. Evidence is largely descriptive/observational, not RCT.
criticisms: Three main critique families exist. (1) Empirical effectiveness: critics including the Behavioral Scientist (article 'Hooked Is Wrong') argue that successful habit-forming products win because they easily and enjoyably solve a problem, not because they apply Eyal's checklist; the model is an after-the-fact narrative. (2) Skinner-box overgeneralization: applying variable-ratio schedules from pigeon studies to consumer software conflates compulsive engagement with genuine value, and ignores Skinner's own scope conditions; humans are not rats and have meta-awareness of being manipulated. (3) Ethical critique (Per Axbom, Tristan Harris, Center for Humane Technology, James Williams 'Stand Out of Our Light'): Hooked teaches designers to engineer compulsion, then deflects responsibility to users via 'self-control' framing in Indistractable - paralleling tobacco-industry rhetoric. Wikipedia notes Eyal has publicly opposed regulation of habit-forming tech on personal-responsibility grounds. Additional limits: the Manipulation Matrix is a self-administered ethics test with no external audit; the model assumes hedonic users and underweights deliberate/reflective use cases (research, creative work, study); Novelty Effect can mask whether Hook-driven engagement is durable or just early-adopter spike.
failure_cases: Documented backlash and concrete failures linked to Hooked-style design: Snapchat Streaks famously created teen anxiety and FOMO (multiple Atlantic / NYT pieces 2017-2019), with documented cases of friends taking over each other's accounts to maintain streaks during hospitalization. Instagram likes were hidden in selected markets in 2019 after evidence linking like-counts to teen mental-health harms (Adam Mosseri). Facebook's autoplay video / News Feed ranking was rolled back for 'Time Well Spent' in 2018 after Mark Zuckerberg's January 2018 announcement that meaningful interactions would be prioritized over passive engagement, a direct response to Tristan Harris's critique of Hooked-style design. Duolingo faced backlash in 2022-2024 over guilt-tripping notifications ('Duo will cry') that became a meme and were partially toned down. Apple introduced Screen Time (2018) and Google introduced Digital Wellbeing (2018) explicitly as countermeasures. Eyal himself shifted positioning to 'Indistractable' (2019), acknowledging that the 2014 framing did not adequately address the user-side cost.
antipatterns: Implementations to avoid: (1) Streak loss with shame framing ('you broke your 47-day streak') is a Means-Ends Reversal where the streak becomes the goal, not the learning. (2) Dark-pattern triggers: confirmshaming ('No thanks, I don't want to learn'), notification spam, fake red badges. (3) Variable rewards detached from genuine value (slot-machine pulls, mystery loot boxes for content access) - Eyal himself classifies these as Dealer-quadrant. (4) Investment hooks that exploit sunk cost: huge friend graphs, non-portable progress, contact-import lock-in. (5) Internal-trigger hijacking on negative emotions (loneliness, anxiety) as the primary acquisition surface - regulatory risk in EU DSA (2024) and the UK Online Safety Act for products targeting under-18s. (6) Burying the unsubscribe / pause behind multiple screens - violates the GDPR/CCPA dark-pattern ban and Apple App Store Review Guideline 4.7. (7) For learning products specifically: gamifying speed over comprehension, awarding XP for shallow correct answers without retention checks, and notification-driven study sessions that condition the learner to study only when prompted (the opposite of intrinsic-motivation development).
applicability_to_shikaq: Useful as a *diagnostic checklist*, dangerous as a *design north star*. shikaq's 20s-female cafe-study persona is exactly the demographic that has become wary of manipulative streak/notification design (see Snapchat/Duolingo backlash). Apple HIG-all-in further constrains the trigger arsenal: aggressive push notifications and red badges conflict with Apple's Notification design guidance and Liquid Glass restraint. Recommended translation: keep the four-step structure but execute it in the Facilitator quadrant of the Manipulation Matrix only. (1) Trigger: morning calendar widget + lock-screen Live Activity showing 'today's 5 questions' (low-pressure, dismissible, never red-badge nag); evening optional audio-quiz Live Activity. (2) Action: one-tap 'Start' on TodayContent with a single primary question card (Apple Journal aesthetic, no choice paralysis). (3) Variable Reward: focus on Rewards of the Self (mastery feedback - 'you'll remember this until [date]', Apple Fitness rings closing) and Rewards of the Hunt (next question reveal, audio narration unlock); minimize Rewards of the Tribe at launch (no leaderboards, no public streaks - they conflict with cafe-study privacy and the Apple HIG aesthetic). (4) Investment: bookmarks, AI-Q&A history, personalized 'wrong-answer notebook', and FSRS card-state - all create switching cost via genuine value, not sunk cost. HEXAD mapping: this serves Achievers (mastery rewards), Philanthropists (the framework respects them via Facilitator framing), Free Spirits (pause without penalty); explicitly do NOT lean on Socializers or Disruptors (incompatible with the persona). Hard rule: shikaq must publish a Manipulation-Matrix self-assessment internally and reject any feature that lands outside Facilitator (e.g. guilt notifications, streak-loss shame, dark-pattern paywalls).
phase_assignment: Phase 1 (3 weeks) for the structural Trigger->Action->Variable Reward->Investment scaffolding (morning trigger card, one-tap Start, FSRS-driven mastery reward, bookmark/notes investment). Phase 2 (4-6 weeks) for richer variable-reward variety (audio quiz unlocks, completion animations, AI-Q&A integration). Phase 3 deliberately holds back on Tribe rewards (leaderboards/social) until persona research validates them; may stay permanently out of scope.
integration_points: Components: TodayContent (renders the daily Trigger card + one-tap Action), Practice screen (Action surface + FSRS-driven Reward of the Self), Summary / Apple Fitness rings (closing-the-ring as Variable Reward), Sidebar (subtle due-count chip, never red badge), AIQA and Bookmarks (Investment), MobileToday Live Activity (Phase 2 Trigger). DB: existing `progress`, `bookmarks`, `quiz_results`, `ai_qa_history` already cover the Investment side; add a small `triggers` table (user_id, trigger_type: 'morning'|'evening'|'streak_resume', scheduled_at, fired_at, dismissed) for analytics-only telemetry on which triggers fire. Notifications: use APNs Live Activities + a daily local notification at user-chosen time (Settings -> Reminders), never marketing pushes. RLS: per-user as standard; admin can read aggregate trigger metrics for Manipulation-Matrix audit. Edge Functions: a daily cron that queues the morning trigger; a Manipulation-Matrix lint Edge Function that flags features adding more than N notifications per week per user.
risks: Ethical: this is the single highest-risk theory in the entire research set. Naive application produces Snapchat-streak / Duolingo-guilt patterns that directly conflict with shikaq's 'Apple if Apple made a study app' brand and would alienate the cafe-study persona. Brand: Apple HIG culture publicly deprecates manipulative engagement design (WWDC22 Focus, Screen Time framing); Apple App Store Review may reject aggressive trigger patterns under Guideline 4.7 / 5.1.1(v). Persona: 20s-female career-track learners are post-Instagram-trauma and read streak design as childish or coercive - market research signal is negative on overt gamification. Implementation: the model is deceptively simple; the hard part is the editorial discipline to *not* ship every variable-reward idea, requiring a Manipulation-Matrix gate in PR review. Scope creep: leaderboards, social hooks, and gacha-like reward boxes will be proposed by gamification thinking and must be explicitly out-of-scope until evidence demands them. Regulatory: EU DSA Art. 25 (dark patterns ban, in force 2024) and Japan's amended Consumer Contract Law (2023) raise the legal floor for guilt-trip notifications and auto-renewing streaks.
apple_hig_compatibility: needs_adaptation. The Hooked Model is *philosophically* compatible (it is design-pattern-agnostic) but its canonical implementations (notification spam, red badges, streak shame, leaderboards) are HIG-hostile. Adaptation required: replace external triggers with calendar widgets / Live Activities (Apple-blessed), replace Rewards of the Tribe with Rewards of the Self (Apple Fitness rings), use Liquid Glass for non-intrusive trigger surfaces, follow Apple Notifications guidance (relevant, timely, actionable, never marketing), and drop the dark-pattern arsenal entirely. The Manipulation-Matrix Facilitator quadrant is the only HIG-compatible operating point.
ethical_score: negative as commonly implemented; can be neutral-to-positive under strict Manipulation-Matrix Facilitator constraints. Default association is with attention-extraction design (Tristan Harris / Center for Humane Technology critiques explicitly cite Hooked). Burnout/dependency risk is real: streak mechanics + variable rewards demonstrably elevate compulsive checking. shikaq must therefore actively de-fang the model: cap notifications, never use streak-loss shame, never expose public leaderboards, always offer a one-tap 'pause without penalty', and publish an internal Manipulation-Matrix audit per feature. Time-after-time-regret is the specific antipattern to monitor (Center for Humane Technology metric).
evidence_quality: theoretical / company_case_study. The model itself is a synthesis - no rigorous RCT validates the four-step loop as a causal mechanism; published evidence is descriptive case studies (Eyal's blog, MDPI 2023, ResearchGate 2016 Fogg+Hook study) and product-team retention metrics with major confounding (Duolingo streak claims). The underlying psychological inputs are stronger: Skinner's variable-ratio schedules are well-replicated in animal models, Schultz 1997 dopamine prediction-error has neuroscience consensus, Lally et al. 2010 habit formation is a single observational study widely overcited. Treat the Hooked Model as a useful taxonomy, not validated science.
localization_fit_japan: medium. Japan has a strong gacha / variable-reward cultural baseline (mobile gaming dominance, train-commute mobile use), and streak/login-bonus mechanics are baseline-expected in Japanese consumer apps. However, the 20s-female career-track persona shikaq targets has explicitly pulled away from compulsive consumer apps and toward 'life optimization' brands (Studyplus, MIMI, Noom-style) that emphasize self-mastery over streak shame. Notification-heavy implementations will read as 'cheap' or 'soshage-like' (mobile-game-like) and conflict with the cafe-study persona's premium aspiration. The Investment loop (notebook, bookmarks, personalization) translates well; the Trigger and Tribe-reward elements need heavy adaptation to Japanese politeness norms (no aggressive push, opt-in only, soft language). Verdict: the Investment phase is a high-fit pillar; Trigger / Variable Reward need careful Apple-aesthetic restraint to avoid the soshage trap.
